title: 69th New York Volunteers ("Fighting 69th")
note: Colonel Michael Corcoran was held as a Confederate prisoner of war from July 1861 to August 1862. Following his release, his reception and entertainment at the Cooper Shop Volunteer Refreshment Saloon was remembered by the Committee as one of its most marked wartime moments.
source: Philadelphia Inquirer, Nov. 8, 1865 — Cooper Shop Committee closing address

title: 84th New York Infantry ("Brooklyn Rifles")
meta: Col. T. A. Dodge · Dec 2, 1861 · 750 men
note: The 84th New York, known as the "Brooklyn Rifles," was commanded by Col. T. A. Dodge. 750 men were fed at the Union saloon on December 2, 1861.

title: Union Ledger — Dec 2, 1861
note: A single-line ledger entry recording that 750 men of the 84th New York were served on this date.

title: "18 Contrabands & Officers" — Dec 20, 1861
note: A ledger entry recording that the saloon served 18 enslaved or formerly enslaved people traveling with officers — the only entry of its kind found so far in this stretch of the record.

title: 101st New York Infantry
note: 1,038 men of the 101st New York were fed under Col. Jardella's command.

title: 95th New York Infantry
note: 915 men of the 95th New York passed through the day after the 101st New York, under Col. Biddle.

title: 1st Independent Battery, NY Light Artillery (Auburn, NY)
note: A light artillery battery from Auburn, New York passed through Philadelphia in late 1861. Sources disagree on their captain's name — one of the small mysteries still open in the ledger.

title: 116th Pennsylvania Infantry
note: The 116th Pennsylvania was fed outbound in September 1862, one of many Pennsylvania regiments that passed through their own state's saloon on the way to the front.
source: Cooper Shop Ledger, Sept. 2, 1862 (cross-referenced against a regimental history)

title: 121st Pennsylvania Infantry
note: The 121st Pennsylvania passed through on its way home in June 1865, near the very end of the saloons' wartime service.
source: Cooper Shop Ledger, June 4, 1865 (cross-referenced against a regimental history)

title: 93rd New York Infantry — midnight arrival
meta: Mar 7, 1862 · fifth regiment fed that night
note: The 93rd New York arrived at midnight and was fed as the fifth regiment that saloon served that night alone — a glimpse of just how busy a single evening could get.
source: Charles W. Roberts, Personal Recollections of the 93rd New York (1895)

title: 103rd New York "Seward Infantry" — telegraphed ahead for coffee
note: Before arriving at 1 a.m., the 103rd New York telegraphed ahead with a specific request: hot coffee. The ledger keeper noted it as a memorable touch of advance planning.

title: 100th New York — a gift back to the saloon
meta: Mar 10, 1862 · 860 men · Col. James M. Brown
note: On departure, the 100th New York donated 1,500 loaves of bread and 3 barrels of meat back to the saloon that had just fed them.

title: 9th New Jersey — outbound
note: The 9th New Jersey landed at Camden at dusk and marched directly to the Cooper Shop for a meal before continuing on. The regiment would return twice more before the war's end.
source: J. Madison Drake, History of the Ninth New Jersey (1889)

title: 6th New Hampshire
note: 1,080 men of the 6th New Hampshire were fed at the Cooper Shop, later recalled by a veteran of the regiment who named Cooper himself as "the patriotic originator of that retreat."
source: Jackman, History of the Sixth New Hampshire (1891)

title: William M. Cooper
meta: Founder & President, Cooper Shop Refreshment Saloon
note: Named as founder in multiple contemporary sources and confirmed independently by his son's 1884 letter. A handful of later accounts give his name as "Erasmus W. Cooper" or "William H. Cooper" instead — possibly a different person, such as the building's owner.

title: Anna M. Ross
meta: Lady Principal, Cooper Shop Volunteer Hospital · d. c. Dec 23, 1863
note: Lady Principal of the Cooper Shop Hospital from its opening. Signed fundraising correspondence as "Anna M. Ross, Lady Principal." Died at approximately age 50 and was buried at Monument Cemetery; succeeded by Mrs. Abigail Horner.
source: Moore, The Rebellion Record (1866), Chapter IV

title: Mrs. Abigail Horner
meta: Lady Principal, Cooper Shop Hospital (succeeded Ross)
note: Served nearly four years as Lady Principal of the Cooper Shop Hospital following Anna M. Ross's death. Filed a final report on September 9, 1865.
source: Moore, The Rebellion Record (1866), Chapter IV

title: Robert R. Corson
meta: Union saloon field operative, self-styled "Chief Pumper"
note: Corson operated a coal office at 133 Walnut St. with partner Chas. Bacon, and signed his UVRS field reports "Chief Pumper" or "C.P." — a self-deprecating title that reveals the saloon's informal internal culture.

title: "The great apology for war"
meta: N. P. Hallowell to R. R. Corson · Dec 3, 1861
note: Writing six weeks after Ball's Bluff, Hallowell called the UVRS's humanitarian work "the great apology for war," reflecting that war "intensifies the brutal passions of men" but also "developes to an unwonted degree all that is noble and Good in our nature." He mentions his sister Anna Hallowell as an active UVRS volunteer.
source: HSP Fales Collection letter, IMG_4661–4677 range

title: Colonel Corcoran's homecoming reception
note: On his release from Confederate imprisonment, Col. Michael Corcoran of the 69th NY was received and entertained at the Cooper Shop — an event the Committee later singled out in its own closing address as one of its most memorable moments of service.

title: Mrs. Maria Atkinson, Mrs. Ellen G. Deaney, Mrs. Catharine Gaw
meta: Last three surviving members, Ladies' Committee of Fifteen (as of 1906)
note: Honored by the Anna M. Ross G.A.R. Post at Mechanics' Cemetery on Memorial Day 1906 with gold medals. Mrs. Atkinson was identified in the article as William M. Cooper's daughter, then age 65.

title: Miss Clara T. Cooper
meta: Daughter of William M. Cooper · d. before Jan 6, 1863
note: Described in committee resolutions as "one of the original, though the youngest member" of the Ladies' Committee — and, on account of her long service relative to her age, also called "one of the oldest members." The resolution on her death was signed by her father as Committee President.
source: Moore, The Rebellion Record (1866), Chapter III

title: Union saloon founding date: May 27 or June 1, 1861?
note: A commemorative 1901 token inscribes May 27, 1861 as the founding date. Rebellion Record Vol. 4 independently yields the same May 27 date plus a December 1861 committee roster, while other sources point to June 1. Current working theory: two saloons opened on consecutive days, not one date recorded two ways — but this is not yet confirmed by a single contemporaneous document naming both dates together.

title: Exterior of the Cooper Shop, 1862
caption: "EXTERIOR OF THE COOPER SHOP REFRESHMENT SALOON, FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT OF THE UNION VOLUNTEERS ON THEIR WAY TO WASHINGTON."
note: The earliest confirmed dated engraving of the Cooper Shop building, published the same week as a companion interior view of the Union saloon. "WM. M. COOPER" is legible on the facade, with a flag reading "WILLIAM M. COOPER" flying above stacked rifles and a crowd of soldiers.

title: Interior of the Union Saloon, 1862
meta: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper · Sept. 27, 1862 · sketch by F. H. Schell
caption: "INTERIOR OF THE UNION REFRESHMENT SALOON, CORNER OF WASHINGTON AND SWANSON STREETS, PHILADELPHIA."
note: Published the same week as the Cooper Shop exterior view, under the joint headline "National Refreshment Saloons in Philadelphia" — a rare look inside the dining hall itself, sketched on site by staff artist F. H. Schell.

title: "The Cooper Shop"
caption: "THE COOPER SHOP."
note: The clearest known exterior view of the building: a canvas banner reading "Cooper Shop Volunteer Refreshment Saloon Free," Cooper's own cooperage business legible on the adjacent structure, masts and a steamboat smokestack from the Washington Avenue wharf behind it, and a crowd filling the street.
source: Enclosure to a 3rd New Hampshire veteran's letter, 1893

title: The Cooper Shop and Hospital in 1864
caption: "THE COOPER-SHOP VOLUNTEER REFRESHMENT SALOON AND HOSPITAL IN 1864."
note: Shows the Soldiers Hospital and the Cooper Shop side by side. The facade also reads "Baptist Bethel" above Cooper's name — an unresolved question about whether the saloon occupied a former church building.

title: Period photograph of the Union saloon
note: One of the clearest surviving period photographs of an intact Union saloon building, with the roofline sign fully legible and railroad tracks running through the foreground — physical proof of how close the saloon sat to the rail line it served.

title: The Union saloon and hospital, hand-colored
note: A rare hand-colored image showing the full Union saloon and hospital complex — a carved and gilded eagle on the roof peak, a cannon on display out front, and a crowd of soldiers gathered below.

title: Edward Moran's winter view, 1865
caption: "WELCOME TO THE BRAVE VOLUNTEERS!"
note: A painting by Edward Moran shows the saloon in snow, with the patriotic mottoes painted on its walls — "Your country calls," "Brave, your deeds for liberty will never die!" — text that survives nowhere else, since it doesn't appear in any photograph.

title: Bird's-eye view with the full roster
caption: "VIEW OF THE UNION VOLUNTEER REFRESHMENT SALOON, PHILADELPHIA, WHERE OVER 300,000 UNION SOLDIERS HAVE BEEN FED."
note: Pairs an engraved bird's-eye view with the fullest known list of officers and committee members — including both Robert R. Corson and "E. W. Cooper," a figure distinct from the Cooper Shop's William M. Cooper.
